narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually above the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset—and your plan to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your probability of failing at 52.7%, tipping the decision toward rejection to ensure we select students with the highest predicted success rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 28.50, well below the average of 36.70, which carried the strongest weight in the model's assessment for you. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the next biggest impact, but in a favorable way, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's stronger than most. Your Year 1 school decile of 5—slightly below the average of 5.71—also helped by nudging things toward success, though less powerfully. Your middle family income bracket played a smaller role in increasing the failure risk, while your Year 3 decile of 4 had a minor push toward failure, and your full-time status offered a slight positive lift. Compared to typical students, your LSAT stands out as the biggest gap, while your GPA is a real highlight that sets you apart favorably. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats to protect program outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it could dramatically shift your prediction. Strengthening early-year deciles through additional academic prep might help too. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these key areas; your story and resilience deserve another shot. Keep pushing—you've got this.
